title: Anthropology
intro: Anthropology questions in this unit cluster heavily around a small canon of theorists and their signature fieldwork. Claude Levi-Strauss and Bronislaw Malinowski are the two most-asked answers, followed closely by Ruth Benedict, Clifford Geertz, Franz Boas, and Margaret Mead. Boas's Columbia circle — Benedict, Mead, Kroeber, and later Zora Neale Hurston and Gene Weltfish — dominates the American side of the canon, while Malinowski, Radcliffe-Brown, Evans-Pritchard, and Edmund Leach anchor a distinct British tradition built on intensive fieldwork among the Trobriand islanders, the Nuer, and other African and Melanesian peoples. Marcel Mauss and Levi-Strauss represent a separate French line running through kinship, exchange, and structuralism.

A second major cluster consists of the works and concepts these theorists produced: Patterns of Culture, The Chrysanthemum and the Sword, Coming of Age in Samoa, Argonauts of the Western Pacific, and The Golden Bough recur as titles, alongside common-link concept answerlines such as culture, magic, kinship, marriage, taboo, potlatch, gift giving, and the Kula ring. Symbolic and interpretive approaches, represented by Geertz's "thick description" and Balinese cockfight essay and Mary Douglas's work on taboo and pollution, form a recognizable sub-cluster alongside the culture-and-personality tradition of Benedict, Mead, and Bateson.

A smaller but consistent presence is biological and evolutionary anthropology, covering Olduvai Gorge, Neanderthals, Homo erectus, and primatologists Jane Goodall and Dian Fossey, as well as materialist and political-economic anthropology represented by Marvin Harris, Marshall Sahlins, David Graeber, and Napoleon Chagnon's studies of the Yanomamo. Contemporary theory appears through Arjun Appadurai, Bruno Latour, Erving Goffman, and Michael Taussig. Geographic and ethnic-group answerlines — the United States, Indonesia, Brazil, Haiti, the Kalahari Desert, Australian Aborigines, and the Inuit — round out the canon as the peoples and places most often tied to these theorists' fieldwork.

sections.name: Boasian Foundations and Cultural Evolutionism
sections.blurb: The late-19th- and early-20th-century founders who established cultural relativism against earlier unilinear evolutionist schemes.

entries.note: German-American founder of cultural relativism and four-field anthropology; mentored Benedict, Mead, and Kroeber at Columbia.
Core anthropological concept theorized as a learned, patterned, relativistic way of life by Boas, Tylor, and Kroeber.
Holistic study of humankind spanning cultural, biological, linguistic, and archaeological subfields, founded by Boas and Tylor.
American Boasian anthropologist at Berkeley who studied California Native peoples and befriended Ishi, the last Yahi.
Last surviving member of the Yahi people of California; lived at Kroeber's Berkeley museum after 1911.
19th-century American evolutionist who studied Iroquois kinship terminology; wrote Ancient Society, influencing Marx and Engels.
James Frazer's comparative study tracing an evolutionary progression from magic to religion to science.
Boas-trained African-American anthropologist and Harlem Renaissance writer who studied Haitian vodou and Southern folklore.
British founder of cultural evolutionism who broadly defined "culture" and theorized animism as religion's origin.
Edward Tylor's 1871 book defining culture and proposing animism as religion's evolutionary origin.
U.S. state where Kroeber documented Native peoples, including Ishi, the last Yahi, at Berkeley's museum.
Boas's principle that cultures should be understood on their own terms rather than judged by outside standards.
Boas-trained American anthropologist who studied Pawnee culture and co-wrote The Races of Mankind against scientific racism.
Boasian practice of urgently documenting cultures believed to be vanishing, as in Kroeber's work on Ishi.

entries.topic: The Golden Bough: A Study in Comparative Religion
entries.answerline: The Golden Bough: A Study in Comparative Religion
entries.note: Full title of James Frazer's comparative study tracing an evolution from magic through religion to science.

sections.name: British Social Anthropology and Functionalist Fieldwork
sections.blurb: Functionalist and structural-functionalist theorists who pioneered long-term participant-observation fieldwork in Melanesia and Africa.

entries.note: Polish-British functionalist who pioneered intensive participant-observation fieldwork among the Trobriand islanders; wrote Argonauts of the Western Pacific.
Ritual practice studied by Malinowski as Trobriand gardening magic, distinguished functionally from religion and science.
Melanesian people off Papua New Guinea studied by Malinowski, famed for the Kula ring gift-exchange system.
Descriptive fieldwork method of long-term participant observation pioneered by Malinowski's Trobriand research.
British structural-functionalist who studied the Andaman Islanders, emphasizing social structure's role in maintaining stability.
British social anthropologist who studied the Nuer and Azande of Sudan, writing on witchcraft and segmentary lineage.
Reciprocal exchange practice central to Malinowski's Trobriand Kula ring fieldwork.
South Sudanese cattle-herding people studied by Evans-Pritchard for segmentary lineage and time-reckoning systems.
Variant name for the British structural-functionalist who studied the Andaman Islanders and social structure's function.
Malinowski's 1922 ethnography establishing participant-observation method, describing the Trobriand Kula ring exchange.
Full name of the Polish-British founder of functionalist fieldwork methodology in the Trobriand Islands.
Melanesian society near the Trobriands studied by Reo Fortune, cited in Benedict's Patterns of Culture as "paranoid."
British social anthropologist who studied Kachin political systems in Highland Burma, oscillating between hierarchy and equality.
Jomo Kenyatta's ethnography of Kikuyu life and custom, written as a student under Malinowski.
Ceremonial gift-exchange system of shell valuables among Trobriand islanders, described in Malinowski's Argonauts of the Western Pacific.

sections.name: American Culture-and-Personality School
sections.blurb: Boas's students who linked culture to individual psychology and national character through fieldwork in Oceania and East Asia.

entries.note: American Boas student who contrasted Apollonian and Dionysian cultures in Patterns of Culture; also wrote The Chrysanthemum and the Sword.
American Boasian ethnographer of adolescence and gender in Samoa and New Guinea; wrote Coming of Age in Samoa.
British-American anthropologist who studied Bali and the Iatmul of New Guinea; coined "schismogenesis"; married to Margaret Mead.
Melanesian fieldwork site for Mead's gender studies and Bateson's Iatmul research on the Sepik River.
Indonesian island studied by Mead and Bateson in Balinese Character, an early photographic ethnography.
Concept theorized by Bateson as a metacommunicative frame, related to Geertz's later "deep play" analysis.
Ruth Benedict's 1946 study of Japanese national character, contrasting shame and guilt cultures.

entries.topic: The Chrysanthemum and the Sword: Patterns of Japanese Culture
entries.answerline: The Chrysanthemum and the Sword: Patterns of Japanese Culture
entries.note: Full title of Ruth Benedict's 1946 wartime study of Japanese culture and national character.
Margaret Mead's 1928 study arguing Samoan adolescence lacked Western storm-and-stress, later disputed by Derek Freeman.
Ruth Benedict's 1934 book comparing Zuni, Kwakiutl, and Dobu societies as integrated cultural "configurations."
Pueblo people of the American Southwest described by Ruth Benedict as an "Apollonian" culture in Patterns of Culture.

sections.name: French Structuralism and Exchange Theory
sections.blurb: French thinkers who modeled kinship, myth, and gift exchange as expressions of an underlying, universal structure.

entries.note: French structuralist who analyzed myth and kinship via binary oppositions; wrote Tristes Tropiques and The Savage Mind.
French sociologist, nephew of Durkheim, who wrote The Gift, analyzing reciprocity and obligation in exchange.
System of descent and marriage relations analyzed structurally by Levi-Strauss as governed by exchange and alliance.
Kinship institution analyzed via incest taboos and exchange theory by Levi-Strauss and structural-functionalists.
Objects of reciprocal exchange theorized by Mauss as carrying obligations to give, receive, and repay.
Taboo universally regulated by kinship rules, theorized by Levi-Strauss as the origin of exchange and alliance.

sections.name: Symbolic and Interpretive Anthropology
sections.blurb: Approaches treating culture as a web of symbols, rituals, and meanings to be interpreted rather than causally explained.

entries.note: American interpretive anthropologist who treated culture as a web of meaning requiring "thick description"; studied Balinese cockfights and Java.

entries.topic: "Deep Play: Notes on the Balinese Cockfight"
entries.answerline: "Deep Play: Notes on the Balinese Cockfight"
entries.note: Clifford Geertz's 1972 essay interpreting Balinese cockfights as symbolic status drama, modeling "thick description."
Indonesian island studied by Geertz in The Religion of Java and his agricultural involution thesis.
British symbolic anthropologist who wrote Purity and Danger, analyzing pollution, taboo, and classificatory boundaries.
Ritual specialists who mediate spirit worlds, studied cross-culturally as healers and religious intermediaries.
Central unit of analysis for interpretive anthropologists like Geertz, who read culture as a system of symbols.
Prohibition concept theorized by Mary Douglas via pollution and boundary-marking in Purity and Danger.
Accused practitioners of harmful magic studied by Evans-Pritchard among the Azande as a coherent belief system.
Folk narratives analyzed structurally and comparatively for recurring motifs, as in Joseph Campbell's monomyth work.
World religion studied anthropologically for ritual practice, veiling, and comparative religious meaning.
American mythologist who proposed the comparative "monomyth" hero's journey across world fairy tales and myths.
Systems of belief and ritual analyzed comparatively, from Frazer's evolutionary scheme to Geertz's symbolic approach.
Patterned symbolic practices analyzed by interpretive anthropologists for the meanings and social functions they enact.
Clifford Geertz's interpretive method of richly contextualizing observed behavior to recover its layered cultural meaning.
Garments studied anthropologically as markers of religious practice, modesty, and gendered social meaning, notably in Islam.

sections.name: Materialist and Political-Economic Anthropology
sections.blurb: Theorists explaining custom through ecology, economy, and power, and later critiques of capitalism, debt, and violence.

entries.note: American anthropologist who studied Pacific chiefdoms and potlatch; proposed hunter-gatherers as the "original affluent society."
American anarchist anthropologist who wrote Debt: The First 5,000 Years; associated with Occupy Wall Street.
Amazonian people of Venezuela and Brazil studied by Napoleon Chagnon, who emphasized their endemic warfare.
Practice debated by materialist anthropologists like Harris over ecological versus symbolic explanations for the custom.
American founder of cultural materialism, explaining customs like sacred cows through ecological and economic causes.
Pacific Northwest Coast gift-giving feast redistributing wealth, analyzed by Boas and later Sahlins.
Theme central to Chagnon's Yanomamo studies and debates over the biological versus cultural origins of human aggression.
Marvin Harris's theory explaining cultural practices as adaptations to material and ecological conditions.
American anthropologist controversial for depicting the Yanomamo as "the fierce people" shaped by chronic warfare.
Subsistence strategy analyzed through materialist ecological anthropology and studies of intensification and involution.

sections.name: Biological and Evolutionary Anthropology
sections.blurb: The study of human physical evolution, extinct hominins, and living primates through fossils, tools, and field observation.

entries.note: Tanzanian site where the Leakeys found early hominin fossils including Homo habilis and Paranthropus boisei.
British primatologist who studied chimpanzee tool use and social behavior at Gombe Stream, Tanzania.
Extinct hominin species that overlapped and interbred with early Homo sapiens in Ice Age Europe.
Continent central to human origins research at sites like Olduvai Gorge and to hominin fossil finds.
American primatologist who studied mountain gorillas in Rwanda; wrote Gorillas in the Mist.
Extinct hominin species that first left Africa, used fire and Acheulean tools, ancestral to later humans.
Material of early hominin tool industries, such as the Acheulean tools associated with Homo erectus.
Skeletal evidence used to reconstruct diet, age, and species identity in fossil hominin analysis.

sections.name: Contemporary and Postmodern Anthropology
sections.blurb: Later-20th- and 21st-century theorists addressing globalization, science studies, and the performance of everyday social life.

entries.note: Indian-American anthropologist who theorized globalization via cultural "scapes" in Modernity at Large.
Canadian-American sociologist who theorized dramaturgical self-presentation in The Presentation of Self in Everyday Life.
Proposed geological epoch of pervasive human ecological impact, addressed by contemporary environmental anthropology.
French science-and-technology-studies scholar who wrote We Have Never Been Modern and developed actor-network theory.
Australian anthropologist known for work on shamanism, colonial violence, and mimesis in South America.

sections.name: Ethnographic Regions and Peoples
sections.blurb: Places and peoples that recur across the unit as canonical fieldwork sites and geographic answerlines.

entries.note: Home institution and fieldwork base for the Boasian and culture-and-personality anthropologists dominating this unit.
Caribbean nation studied by Zora Neale Hurston, whose Tell My Horse documented vodou and folklore.
Southern African desert home to the San, subjects of hunter-gatherer ethnography and affluence debates.
South American nation home to Amazonian peoples including the Yanomamo studied by Chagnon.
Southeast Asian nation encompassing Bali and Java, key fieldwork sites for Mead, Bateson, and Geertz.
Robert Flaherty's 1922 silent documentary depicting, partly staged, the life of an Inuit family.
Indigenous Australian peoples studied for totemism and kinship, influencing Durkheim's and Levi-Strauss's theories.
Arctic peoples of North America depicted in Nanook of the North and studied for cold-climate subsistence adaptation.
Formal name of the Southeast Asian nation encompassing Bali and Java, key fieldwork sites in this unit.

appendix.answer: Purity and Danger: An Analysis of Concepts of Pollution and Taboo
The Myth of the Negro Past
